title: The Anger of Birth Parents
dek: *The anger of birth parents lives in the body long after papers are signed, a heat that moves through generations. It is not pathology. It is testimony. This piece explores the roots of that fury, the systems that feed it, and the practices that can help you witness it without being consumed.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Birth parents carry anger that society rarely acknowledges, a fury born from loss, systemic coercion, and silenced grief. This rage is physiological, psychological, and often intergenerational, demanding recognition before healing can begin. When we name the anger and trace its roots, we create space for birth parents to reclaim their stories.

Have you ever noticed how quickly people change the subject when a birth parent mentions rage? Not sadness. Not the socially acceptable grief that fits neatly into support group pamphlets. The hot, throat-closing, fist-clenching anger that comes when you realize your child is being raised by someone else, and no one asked if you were truly free to choose. In my own practice, I've watched birth mothers describe this anger with their hands first, before words arrive. A tightening across the sternum. A jaw that locks at night. The body knows what happened before the mind can form sentences around it. ## The Coercion No One Calls Coercion Adoption agencies don't typically use force. They use timing. They use economic pressure dressed as counseling. They use the language of "best interest" when a young woman is at her most vulnerable, often alone, often terrified. Beverly Engel's work on shame and anger helps us understand this dynamic. She identifies how systemic shaming, particularly around sexuality and motherhood, creates a breeding ground for what she calls "anger at the self" that is actually anger at the systems that constrained choice. When a birth parent was told she wasn't ready, wasn't stable, wasn't enough, that message didn't come from nowhere. It came from a culture that has always policed who gets to mother. The anger isn't irrational. It's the only rational response to being told your love isn't sufficient while simultaneously being told you're making a loving choice. That cognitive dissonance lives in the nervous system. The NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system shows us how unresolved anger keeps the body in a state of hyperarousal, with elevated cortisol and disrupted sleep architecture. For birth parents, this isn't abstract research. It's Tuesday. ## The Anger That Has No Socially Acceptable Target Here's where it gets complicated. Who do you direct your rage toward when everyone involved believed they were doing the right thing? The adoptive parents who seem like good people? The social worker who was kind? Your own mother who said she'd help but couldn't? The boyfriend who disappeared? The economic system that makes childcare cost more than rent? God? Soraya Chemaly's research on women and anger reveals how girls are socialized to turn anger inward, to transform it into depression, anxiety, or self-harm rather than direct it outward. For birth mothers, this pattern intensifies. You're supposed to be grateful the child has a good home. You're supposed to feel peace about your decision. Anger disrupts that narrative, so it gets pathologized or ignored. But anger without a target doesn't disappear. It circulates. It becomes generalized irritability, chronic tension, a feeling of being unsafe in your own skin. Over the years I've seen birth parents describe a constant low-grade rage they can't quite place, a sense that the world is fundamentally unjust but they can't point to a single villain. That's because the villain isn't a person. It's a system. And systems are hard to punch. ## The Grief That Wears Anger's Clothes Sometimes rage is just grief in a more socially powerful outfit. Picture this: You're at a grocery store, and you see a mother with a child about the age yours would be. Your chest tightens. Your face flushes. You want to scream, but instead you leave your cart in the aisle and walk out. Later, you'll feel ashamed of the intensity. You'll wonder if you're broken. You're not broken. You're mourning. And when grief has no ritual, no communal witness, no permission to exist, it often shows up as anger because anger at least feels like agency. Harriet Lerner's work on the dance of anger emphasizes that anger is often a signal emotion, pointing to something underneath that needs attention. For birth parents, that something is often a loss so profound that the psyche can barely hold it. The anger says: *This matters. This mattered. I matter.* When you explore [the assessments hub](/assessments) on this site, you might find tools for tracking emotional patterns. But know this: your anger doesn't need to be fixed. It needs to be heard. ## The Body Keeps the Signature Your shoulders remember the moment you signed the papers. Even if your conscious mind has made peace, even if you tell yourself it was the right choice, the tissue holds memory. You might notice your jaw clenches when someone mentions adoption casually at a dinner party. You might find your hands shake when you see photos of reunions online. Your lower back might seize up around the anniversary date, year after year, and no amount of stretching seems to help. This is not psychosomatic in the dismissive sense. This is the body doing exactly what bodies do: storing what the nervous system couldn't process in real time. The anger lives in fascia, in the gut, in the places where you couldn't scream or run or fight. Some birth parents find relief in practices that release tension without requiring narrative. Vigorous movement. Cold water immersion. Screaming in the car on a back road. The supplement [Momentous Magtein Magnesium L-Threonate, 30 Servings](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B09WWK4R2C?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support nervous system regulation when the body is stuck in fight-or-flight, though it's not a substitute for the deeper work of witnessing your own rage. ## When Anger Becomes Identity There's a danger here, too. Anger can become so familiar that you mistake it for selfhood. In our experience working with birth parents, there's often a moment when someone realizes they've been performing rage because it's the only emotion that makes them feel real. It's the only feeling that seems proportional to the loss. And while that anger is valid, living inside it constantly is exhausting. This isn't about toxic positivity or premature forgiveness. It's about recognizing when anger has shifted from a messenger to a prison guard. When it's no longer pointing you toward something that needs addressing but instead keeping you in a loop of rehearsed grievances that never quite resolve. You can honor your anger and also notice when it's running on autopilot. Both things can be true. ## The Anger Your Daughter Will Inherit (If You Don't Metabolize It) Anger is contagious across generations. Not through moral failing, but through nervous system attunement. If you're a birth mother who went on to parent other children, they feel your unmetabolized rage even when you think you're hiding it. They sense the tension in your body when certain topics arise. They learn, wordlessly, that there are subjects too dangerous to name. And sometimes, they'll carry a free-floating anger they can't explain, an inheritance they didn't ask for. This isn't to create more shame. You've had enough of that. It's to say: your healing isn't just for you. It's for the lineage. When you do the work of acknowledging, feeling, and moving through your anger, you break a pattern. You teach the next generation that rage can be witnessed without annihilation. That grief can be named without collapse. That you can survive the truth of your own story. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the idea that anger is information. It tells you where a boundary was crossed, where your power was taken, where love was interrupted. For birth parents, that information is sacred. It's the map back to yourself. ## A Practice for Meeting Your Anger You don't need an hour or special equipment. You need five minutes and the willingness to feel something uncomfortable. Sit somewhere you won't be interrupted. Place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Close your eyes if that feels safe. Ask yourself: *Where does the anger live in my body right now?* Don't analyze. Just notice. Is it in your throat? Your fists? Your gut? Breathe into that place. Let it be as big as it is. You might feel heat, pressure, trembling. Stay with it. Say out loud, even if it's just a whisper: "This anger is allowed. This anger is information." If tears come, let them. If you need to punch a pillow, do that. If you just sit there and breathe and notice, that's enough. The practice isn't to make the anger go away. It's to stop making yourself wrong for having it. Some people find that [Tattva's Herbs Ayurvedic Pitta Cooling Massage Oil 8oz](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07Q3T2N6Z?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps afterward, especially when anger manifests as heat in the body. Massage your own hands, your forearms, your feet. Give yourself the touch that says: you survived this. You're still here. ## The Anger That Becomes Medicine There's a version of this story where your anger transforms into something generative. Not because you transcended it or did enough therapy or finally forgave everyone. But because you let it teach you. Some birth parents become advocates, using their rage as fuel to change adoption practices. Some become artists, channeling the heat into work that tells the truth. Some simply learn to say no, to set boundaries, to refuse the narrative that they should be endlessly grateful and quiet. Your anger knows things your polite self will never admit. It knows you deserved support, not separation. It knows the adoption industry profits from vulnerable women. It knows that "open adoption" often closes the moment it's legally allowed to. It knows your child thinks about you, even if you'll never meet. Let your anger make you dangerous to the systems that harmed you. Not violent. Dangerous. The kind of dangerous that tells the truth in rooms that prefer silence. The kind that refuses to shrink. The kind that says: this will not happen to another young mother if I can help it. You can explore more about reclaiming your voice and story at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where we write about the intersection of grief, rage, and the feminine divine. ## Frequently Asked Questions About Birth Parent Anger ### Is it normal to still be angry decades after the adoption? Yes. Grief and anger don't follow a timeline, and adoption loss is what's called "ambiguous loss," a type of mourning without closure. Your anger might shift in texture over the years, but its presence isn't a sign of failure. It's a sign that something profound happened, and your psyche is still integrating it. Some birth parents find the anger actually intensifies after reunion, when the fantasy of what could have been meets the reality of what was lost. ### How do I express anger without damaging my relationship with my child or the adoptive parents? You can have your anger and also choose how you express it. Rage doesn't have to be performed to be real. Many birth parents work with therapists who specialize in adoption issues, or they find peer support groups where anger is normalized. You can scream in your car, write letters you never send, engage in physical practices that let the body release tension. The anger isn't the problem; it's when anger becomes the only language you have that relationships suffer. You're allowed to feel everything and still choose kindness in your interactions. ### What if I feel guilty for being angry when my child seems happy? Your anger and your child's happiness are not mutually exclusive truths. One doesn't cancel out the other. You can be genuinely glad your child is loved and thriving while also mourning what you lost and raging at the systems that made separation seem inevitable. This both/and capacity is part of the complex work of being a birth parent. Guilt often arises when we've internalized the message that we should only feel one way. You're allowed the full spectrum. Consider working with resources in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) that support emotional resilience, like [Pukka Womankind Tea](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005SHNOPS?tag=spankyspinola-20), though no herb can replace the work of self-compassion. ## Where the Heat Goes I think about fire a lot when I work with birth parents. How it can destroy, yes, but also how it clears land for new growth. How it purifies. How it illuminates what was hidden in darkness. Your anger is a kind of fire. It has burned through the polite stories you were told to tell about your experience. It has incinerated the shame that kept you small. And now you stand in the clearing it created, and you have a choice about what to plant there. This isn't the part where I tell you to forgive or let go or find peace. You'll do those things or you won't, in your own time, on your own terms. What I want you to know is that your anger has been trying to protect you all along. It's been saying: *remember. Don't let them make you forget what this cost. Don't let them rewrite your story into something convenient.* So don't. Remember. Let the anger be the keeper of your truth until you're ready to be the keeper yourself. And when you are, when you can hold the full weight of what happened without the heat consuming you, you'll find that the anger hasn't disappeared. It's just become something else. A boundary. A standard. A refusal to be diminished. The kind of power that changes everything it touches, starting with you. --- *For more context on our approach to anger and emotional literacy, visit [the about page](/about) to understand the framework that guides this work.* ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on the anger of birth parents, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
